steps.title: Read the sentence and spot the grammar role
steps.content:
The sentence says Parker’s finding “was the first ______ humans in North America as far back as 10,000 years ago.” The blank follows “the first” and must act as a modifier within that noun phrase. A modifier inside a noun phrase must be nonfinite — not conjugated to a subject.
  • Choice A — ‘places’: a finite, third-person singular verb; cannot modify “first” inside a noun phrase. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘placed’: a finite past-tense form (or past participle producing the illogical “the first placed humans”); does not work as a nonfinite modifier here. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘place’: a finite base form conjugated to a bare subject; also reads as a noun, making “the first place humans” illogical. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘to place’: an infinitive, which is nonfinite, correctly modifying “first” to mean “the first evidence to place humans in North America.” ✓

steps.title: Confirm the answer
steps.content: Only the infinitive “to place” produces a grammatically correct noun phrase with “the first” in Standard English. That matches choice D — D is correct.
